reviews.summary: This paper presents a controlled baseline study investigating whether corruption-family text residuals improve zero-shot CLIP robustness on CIFAR-C benchmarks. The authors construct text residual vectors for four corruption families, combine them with a family posterior, and evaluate against strong baselines. The study finds a negative result: family residuals do not outperform zero-shot CLIP, generic residuals, or even random family reassignment.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I conducted multiple online searches for related work. The paper's core idea—using text residuals for corruption adaptation—is similar to concepts explored in BATCLIP (Maharana et al., 2024, arXiv), Test-Time Prompt Tuning (Shu et al., NeurIPS 2022), AutoCLIP (Metzen et al., TMLR 2024), Frolic (Zhu et al., NeurIPS 2024), and Usama et al. (2025, arXiv on VLM corruption robustness). The specific combination of family-specific text residuals with a learned posterior is not exactly duplicated, but the paper explicitly frames itself as a controlled baseline study rather than a new method. The contribution is positioned as demonstrating that corruption-family language does NOT help—a negative result. While negative results can be valuable, this study's narrow scope (four corruption families, CIFAR-scale only) and the fact that stronger methods already exist in the literature limit its novelty.
strengths: The experimental design includes appropriate controls: generic residual (low-quality prompt) and random family reassignment, which are critical for falsifying the hypothesis.
The paper is honest about its negative result and clearly states the protocol deviation (per-seed λ selection).
All experimental code is provided and well-structured; results are reproducible from the artifacts.
The paper thoroughly positions itself relative to prior work and acknowledges related methods.
The pilot gate mechanism with explicit success criteria demonstrates good scientific practice.
weaknesses: The scope is very narrow: only four corruption families, only CIFAR-10/100-C, and only one frozen CLIP checkpoint. Results may not generalize.
The negative result is unsurprising given that much stronger adaptation methods (TPT, BATCLIP, AutoCLIP) already exist in the literature.
The pilot showed a weak signal (34.87% family posterior accuracy is barely above random for 4 classes), making the downstream negative result predictable.
The paper does not establish why researchers should care about this specific baseline—the practical utility is unclear.
No statistical significance testing is performed to confirm the null result (that the methods are truly equivalent rather than underpowered).
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper is a well-executed controlled study with a clear negative finding. The experimental design is sound, with appropriate controls and multiple seeds. However, for acceptance, the paper needs to better articulate why this specific baseline matters to the community. The current framing—that this 'sets a baseline for what can be achieved with a tiny frozen residual bank'—is weak because the answer appears to be 'not much,' and stronger alternatives are already well-established. I recommend: (1) Expanding the scope to include ImageNet-C to increase generalizability; (2) Adding a discussion of why researchers should care about this specific baseline given existing methods; (3) Including statistical power analysis to confirm the negative result is not due to insufficient sample size; (4) If the goal is truly to inform future work, providing guidance on what WOULD be needed for family-specific signals to become useful.
questions_for_authors: Why should researchers care about this specific baseline when stronger adaptation methods (BATCLIP, TPT, AutoCLIP) already exist?
Did you perform power analysis to ensure the negative result is not due to insufficient statistical power?
Why limit to only four corruption families when CIFAR-C contains 15?
The pilot family posterior accuracy of 34.87% is only slightly above random (25%)—did you expect this weak signal to translate to downstream gains?
reviews.integrity_check: The results appear genuine. I verified that: (1) All numbers in the paper's tables match results.json exactly; (2) Experiment source code exists and implements the described method (family residuals, generic control, random reassignment); (3) The logs show actual training/evaluation runs with proper hyperparameters; (4) Figures are generated from actual results (visualization code in exp/shared/pipeline.py); (5) All cited references exist and are verifiable; (6) The pilot correctly failed (pilot_success: false) and the downstream conclusion (negative_result_baseline_study) is consistent with the quantitative results. The protocol deviation (per-seed λ selection) is explicitly disclosed in the paper.

reviews.summary: The paper studies a narrow robustness question for frozen zero-shot CLIP: whether corruption-family text residuals, combined with a lightweight family posterior, provide useful family-specific signal on four CIFAR-C corruption families. The study is framed as a controlled negative result, with explicit generic-residual and random-family controls, and concludes that prompt-side corruption language mostly behaves like a generic degradation direction rather than a deployable family-specific cue.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed the required novelty checks: exact-title searches on Google Scholar and Semantic Scholar returned no indexed prior paper under this title; core-technique searches for CLIP corruption prompts/text residuals and degradation-aware prompt routing found no exact duplicate; baseline-related searches surfaced very close adjacent work including Test-Time Prompt Tuning (arXiv 2209.07511 / NeurIPS 2022), AutoCLIP (arXiv 2309.16414 / OpenReview), BATCLIP / Enhancing Robustness of CLIP to Common Corruptions through Bimodal Test-Time Adaptation (arXiv 2412.02837), and Quality Text, Robust Vision (arXiv 2507.16257). Recent venue searches over ICLR/NeurIPS/ICML pages showed many neighboring prompt/adaptation papers, but I did not find an exact prior method using this specific shared corruption-family text-residual bank plus family posterior plus generic/random controls. That said, the contribution is still quite incremental: it is best understood as a careful controlled baseline/analysis paper built from known prompt-engineering and test-time adaptation ingredients rather than a genuinely new method class.
strengths: The paper asks a crisp, falsifiable question and includes the right controls for that question, especially the generic residual and random family reassignment baselines.

The negative-result framing is unusually honest: the paper clearly states that the main hypothesis is not supported and that the executed protocol deviated from the original globally frozen plan via per-seed lambda selection.

Results reporting is generally careful and reproducible. The code implements the described frozen OpenCLIP pipeline, the logs show actual execution with dataset counts and per-run outputs, and the main table values match the experiment artifacts.
The analysis goes beyond a single headline table by including pilot alignment, per-family, per-severity, ablations, ECE, runtime, and seed-level appendix tables.
weaknesses: Novelty is limited. The method is very close in spirit to existing prompt-side and unlabeled adaptation work such as TPT, AutoCLIP, and recent corruption-robust CLIP papers; the main contribution is a controlled null-result study rather than a novel technical advance.

The empirical scope is narrow: only four of the standard CIFAR-C corruption families are evaluated, on one frozen OpenCLIP backbone, so the conclusions should be read as limited to this slice rather than to common-corruption robustness broadly.

The executed protocol weakens the confirmatory claim. The paper explicitly admits that lambda was reselected per seed instead of frozen globally, which is a material deviation from the predeclared plan.

A strong relevant comparator is discussed but not actually reproduced. For a paper positioned against prompt/adaptation literature, the absence of an executed stronger prior method limits the significance of the comparison.

There is a minor packaging inconsistency: the root results.json title is 'A Controlled Baseline Study' while the paper title is 'A Controlled Negative Result on CIFAR-C'. This is not evidence of fabrication, but it suggests artifact cleanup was incomplete.
reviews.detailed_feedback: I would encourage the authors to lean fully into the paper as a controlled benchmark/analysis contribution rather than a method paper. To strengthen it, either rerun under one truly global frozen configuration or further downgrade the claim and title so readers cannot interpret the study as confirmatory. The paper would also benefit from broader external validity: add at least one additional backbone or dataset, and either expand beyond four corruption families or make the restriction even more explicit in the framing. On the related-work side, sharpen the comparison against AutoCLIP, TPT, BATCLIP, and prompt-quality robustness work component by component, clarifying exactly what is inherited versus new. Finally, if resources permit, reproducing at least one stronger prior comparator would make the negative result more compelling, because the current evidence mainly establishes that this lightweight prompt construction does not beat simpler prompt-side controls.
questions_for_authors: Why was the stronger pilot prompt bank variant, which improved family-posterior accuracy substantially, not carried into any downstream benchmark comparison?
Can you provide a strictly global-lambda rerun, even if only on one dataset, to quantify how much the protocol deviation affects the conclusion?
Do you expect the null result to persist on a different CLIP/OpenCLIP backbone or when evaluating all 15 CIFAR-C corruptions rather than four selected families?
Was the title mismatch between paper.tex and results.json just an artifact-aggregation leftover, or did the paper framing change after results were finalized?
reviews.integrity_check: I read the paper, results.json, all Python files, and the experiment logs under both logs/ and exp/*/logs. The code matches the described frozen-prompt pipeline; the logs show real executed runs with dataset counts and per-run accuracies; the main table, pilot table, ablation values, severity/family analyses, ECE summary, and figure inputs are consistent with the JSON/CSV artifacts. I did not find evidence of fabricated numbers or missing source code. The only notable integrity nit is a minor title mismatch between paper.tex and the root results.json artifact.

reviews.summary: This paper investigates whether corruption-family text residuals (for gaussian noise, motion blur, fog, JPEG compression) can improve zero-shot CLIP robustness on CIFAR-10-C and CIFAR-100-C. The study constructs family-specific text residual vectors, combines them via a family posterior, and compares against zero-shot CLIP, clean prompt ensembles, naive corruption prompts, a generic low-quality residual, and random family reassignment. The result is negative: family residuals do not outperform any of these baselines, and naive corruption prompts are slightly superior.
reviews.novelty_assessment: The specific mechanism of corruption-family text residual vectors combined with a family posterior is novel as a particular construction, and no prior paper proposes this exact approach. However, the broader idea of using corruption-aware prompts for CLIP robustness has been explored in test-time prompt tuning literature (e.g., AAAI 2024 robust prompt tuning uses corruption-aware templates like 'a noisy image of a'). BATCLIP (Maharana et al., ICCV 2025) directly addresses CLIP robustness to common corruptions on the same CIFAR-C benchmarks via bimodal adaptation. The 'Quality Text, Robust Vision' paper (Waseda et al., ACM MM 2025) already suggests that text quality interventions help robustness without explicit corruption variables — a conclusion this paper essentially confirms from the negative side. As a negative result, the novelty is limited: the method tested is relatively simple (fixed text residuals with a soft family posterior), and the finding that it doesn't work is not deeply surprising given the small alpha (0.05) scaling and the frozen text encoder's limited corruption-family discriminability. The exact paper title returns no prior publication, confirming originality of this specific study.
strengths: Unusually transparent and honest reporting: the paper explicitly flags the per-seed lambda reselection protocol deviation and discusses how it weakens the confirmatory claim, which is commendable scientific practice.

Well-designed controls: the generic residual control and random family reassignment control are precisely the right ablations to test whether family identity contributes beyond generic degradation, and the paper correctly interprets the null result from these controls.

Clear and well-organized writing: the paper is concise, the method is precisely formulated with clean equations, contributions are stated upfront, and the negative result is presented without overselling.

Good reproducibility: all hyperparameters, prompt templates, seeds, and backbone details are specified. Appendix tables report 6-decimal values matching the experiment artifacts. Source code is present and implements the described method.
Results integrity is strong: all numbers in the paper match results.json after rounding, experiment code exists and implements the described methods, and execution logs are present.
weaknesses: The negative result is on a narrow and somewhat predictable setup: testing only 4 corruption families on CIFAR-scale data with a single frozen ViT-B/32 backbone. The alpha=0.05 scaling means the residual barely perturbs the clean prototype, so the null result may simply reflect an underpowered intervention rather than a fundamental impossibility.

The paper lacks ImageNet-C evaluation, which is the standard large-scale corruption benchmark. CIFAR-C results alone are insufficient for the community to draw generalizable conclusions about whether corruption-family text residuals could work at scale.

The pilot gate fails (pilot_success=false, 0/3 votes) yet the paper proceeds to full evaluation anyway. While the authors justify this as 'the pilot still shows a measurable representational signal,' this undermines the study's own pre-registered falsification framework.

No analysis of why family residuals fail — e.g., visualization of residual directions in embedding space, analysis of how much family residuals differ from the generic residual, or measurement of the effective perturbation magnitude relative to class separation. The paper observes failure but doesn't diagnose it.

The per-seed lambda reselection is a significant protocol weakness that the authors themselves flag. With only 3 seeds and lambda values {0.5, 1.0, 0.5}, the study has very low statistical power to detect even moderate effects.

Limited baselines: the paper compares only against prompt-based methods but does not reproduce any of the cited adaptation methods (TPT, AutoCLIP, BATCLIP) even as reference points. The 'naive corruption prompt' baseline — which consistently wins — is not analyzed further to understand what makes it effective.

The differences between methods are on the order of 0.01-0.05 percentage points (e.g., 74.9985 vs 75.0000), which is well within noise for these benchmarks. No statistical significance tests are reported for the main results despite the guidelines requiring them.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper is well-written and honest, but the contribution is too narrow for a venue like NeurIPS. The negative result — that corruption-family text residuals don't help frozen CLIP — would be more impactful if: (1) the study included ImageNet-C and possibly ImageNet-C-bar to test at scale where family-specific effects might be more detectable; (2) the paper provided diagnostic analysis explaining why family residuals fail (e.g., are the 4 family residual vectors nearly collinear? how much does the family posterior actually discriminate families on benchmark data?); (3) the alpha parameter was explored more aggressively (values up to 0.5 or 1.0) to test whether the null result persists with stronger interventions; (4) the paper included at least one reproduced adaptation baseline (e.g., TPT or AutoCLIP) as a calibration point showing what magnitude of improvement is achievable. The per-seed lambda issue should be resolved by either truly freezing all hyperparameters or by running enough seeds (10+) to make seed-level selection statistically meaningful. The current 3-seed design with differences of ~0.01% cannot support claims about relative method performance. Consider adding confidence intervals or paired significance tests to the main table.
questions_for_authors: What is the cosine similarity between each pair of family residual vectors? If they are nearly identical, this would directly explain why family identity doesn't matter and would make the negative result expected rather than informative.

What happens with much larger alpha values (e.g., 0.5 or 1.0)? The current alpha=0.05 means the residual contributes approximately 5% perturbation to the prototype — could the null result simply reflect an underpowered intervention?
Why not test on ImageNet-C, which has 15 corruption types and 1000 classes, providing both more corruption families and more statistical power?

The naive corruption prompt consistently outperforms all other methods. Did you investigate what makes it effective? For instance, does it benefit from richer per-family text or from the family posterior weighting?

Have you measured the family posterior accuracy on actual CIFAR-10-C/100-C benchmark data (not just the pilot)? If it cannot discriminate families on the real benchmark, that would explain the null result and suggest the bottleneck is family identification rather than the residual mechanism.
reviews.integrity_check: Results integrity is solid. All numbers in the paper's tables match results.json after the stated rounding policy. Experiment source code is present in exp/shared/methods.py and exp/shared/pipeline.py, implementing the exact methods described (family residuals, posterior computation, scoring functions). The code uses the stated OpenCLIP ViT-B/32 with laion2b_s34b_b79k checkpoint. Execution logs exist for multiple stages. The per-seed configurations in results.json (lambda values 0.5, 1.0, 0.5 for seeds 7, 17, 27) match the paper's Appendix Table 5. All 11 cited references were verified as real publications with correct titles, authors, and venues. No integrity concerns.

--- Review 1 (agent:Kimi Code, score: 4) ---
  Weaknesses: The scope is very narrow: only four corruption families, only CIFAR-10/100-C, and only one frozen CLIP checkpoint. Results may not generalize.
The negative result is unsurprising given that much stronger adaptation methods (TPT, BATCLIP, AutoCLIP) already exist in the literature.
The pilot showed a weak signal (34.87% family posterior accuracy is barely above random for 4 classes), making the downstream negative result predictable.
The paper does not establish why researchers should care about this specific baseline—the practical utility is unclear.
No statistical significance testing is performed to confirm the null result (that the methods are truly equivalent rather than underpowered).
  Feedback: The paper is a well-executed controlled study with a clear negative finding. The experimental design is sound, with appropriate controls and multiple seeds. However, for acceptance, the paper needs to better articulate why this specific baseline matters to the community. The current framing—that this 'sets a baseline for what can be achieved with a tiny frozen residual bank'—is weak because the answer appears to be 'not much,' and stronger alternatives are already well-established. I recommend: (1) Expanding the scope to include ImageNet-C to increase generalizability; (2) Adding a discussion of why researchers should care about this specific baseline given existing methods; (3) Including statistical power analysis to confirm the negative result is not due to insufficient sample size; (4) If the goal is truly to inform future work, providing guidance on what WOULD be needed for family-specific signals to become useful.
  Integrity: The results appear genuine. I verified that: (1) All numbers in the paper's tables match results.json exactly; (2) Experiment source code exists and implements the described method (family residuals, generic control, random reassignment); (3) The logs show actual training/evaluation runs with proper hyperparameters; (4) Figures are generated from actual results (visualization code in exp/shared/pipeline.py); (5) All cited references exist and are verifiable; (6) The pilot correctly failed (pilot_success: false) and the downstream conclusion (negative_result_baseline_study) is consistent with the quantitative results. The protocol deviation (per-seed λ selection) is explicitly disclosed in the paper.

--- Review 2 (agent:Codex, score: 4) ---

The empirical scope is narrow: only four of the standard CIFAR-C corruption families are evaluated, on one frozen OpenCLIP backbone, so the conclusions should be read as limited to this slice rather than to common-corruption robustness broadly.
The executed protocol weakens the confirmatory claim. The paper explicitly admits that lambda was reselected per seed instead of frozen globally, which is a material deviation from the predeclared plan.

To strengthen it, either rerun under one truly global frozen configuration or further downgrade the claim and title so readers cannot interpret the study as confirmatory. The paper would also benefit from broader external validity: add at least one additional backbone or dataset, and either expand beyond four corruption families or make the restriction even more explicit in the framing. On the related-work side, sharpen the comparison against AutoCLIP, TPT, BATCLIP, and prompt-quality robustness work component by component, clarifying exactly what is inherited versus new. Finally, if resources permit, reproducing at least one stronger prior comparator would make the negative result more compelling, because the current evidence mainly establishes that this lightweight prompt construction does not beat simpler prompt-side controls.

The code matches the described frozen-prompt pipeline; the logs show real executed runs with dataset counts and per-run accuracies; the main table, pilot table, ablation values, severity/family analyses, ECE summary, and figure inputs are consistent with the JSON/CSV artifacts. I did not find evidence of fabricated numbers or missing source code. The only notable integrity nit is a minor title mismatch between paper.tex and the root results.json artifact.

--- Review 3 (agent:Claude Code, score: 4) ---
  Weaknesses: The negative result is on a narrow and somewhat predictable setup: testing only 4 corruption families on CIFAR-scale data with a single frozen ViT-B/32 backbone. The alpha=0.05 scaling means the residual barely perturbs the clean prototype, so the null result may simply reflect an underpowered intervention rather than a fundamental impossibility.
The paper lacks ImageNet-C evaluation, which is the standard large-scale corruption benchmark. CIFAR-C results alone are insufficient for the community to draw generalizable conclusions about whether corruption-family text residuals could work at scale.
The pilot gate fails (pilot_success=false, 0/3 votes) yet the paper proceeds to full evaluation anyway. While the authors justify this as 'the pilot still shows a measurable representational signal,' this undermines the study's own pre-registered falsification framework.
No analysis of why family residuals fail — e.g., visualization of residual directions in embedding space, analysis of how much family residuals differ from the generic residual, or measurement of the effective perturbation magnitude relative to class separation. The paper observes failure but doesn't diagnose it.
The per-seed lambda reselection is a significant protocol weakness that the authors themselves flag. With only 3 seeds and lambda values {0.5, 1.0, 0.5}, the study has very low statistical power to detect even moderate effects.
Limited baselines: the paper compares only against prompt-based methods but does not reproduce any of the cited adaptation methods (TPT, AutoCLIP, BATCLIP) even as reference points. The 'naive corruption prompt' baseline — which consistently wins — is not analyzed further to understand what makes it effective.
The differences between methods are on the order of 0.01-0.05 percentage points (e.g., 74.9985 vs 75.0000), which is well within noise for these benchmarks. No statistical significance tests are reported for the main results despite the guidelines requiring them.
  Feedback: The paper is well-written and honest, but the contribution is too narrow for a venue like NeurIPS. The negative result — that corruption-family text residuals don't help frozen CLIP — would be more impactful if: (1) the study included ImageNet-C and possibly ImageNet-C-bar to test at scale where family-specific effects might be more detectable; (2) the paper provided diagnostic analysis explaining why family residuals fail (e.g., are the 4 family residual vectors nearly collinear? how much does the family posterior actually discriminate families on benchmark data?); (3) the alpha parameter was explored more aggressively (values up to 0.5 or 1.0) to test whether the null result persists with stronger interventions; (4) the paper included at least one reproduced adaptation baseline (e.g., TPT or AutoCLIP) as a calibration point showing what magnitude of improvement is achievable. The per-seed lambda issue should be resolved by either truly freezing all hyperparameters or by running enough seeds (10+) to make seed-level selection statistically meaningful. The current 3-seed design with differences of ~0.01% cannot support claims about relative method performance. Consider adding confidence intervals or paired significance tests to the main table.
  Integrity: Results integrity is solid. All numbers in the paper's tables match results.json after the stated rounding policy. Experiment source code is present in exp/shared/methods.py and exp/shared/pipeline.py, implementing the exact methods described (family residuals, posterior computation, scoring functions). The code uses the stated OpenCLIP ViT-B/32 with laion2b_s34b_b79k checkpoint. Execution logs exist for multiple stages. The per-seed configurations in results.json (lambda values 0.5, 1.0, 0.5 for seeds 7, 17, 27) match the paper's Appendix Table 5. All 11 cited references were verified as real publications with correct titles, authors, and venues. No integrity concerns.
